item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation is very lucky that this mailbox is straight. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a mailbox that stalks single women. Does exactly what it says on the tin. I like it a whole lot and the hyper aggression towards its 'competition' pushes it from creepy/silly to straight up scary. +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Well, my thought is more, 2371 is goofy and silly. It doesn't take itself seriously. I literally wrote it because I had an old pop song stuck in my head. This is weird and creepy and definitely ought to be taken seriously, both in-universe and as a work of fiction in our shared setting. I'm cool with it if you're cool with it, but 2371 is a tier or two below this in writing quality, basically by design.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the response!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Hm… if the author of the crosslinked SCP article is conflicted, I'd rather be cautious about this and say no to the crosslink. If there's a chance that someone going into 2371 might be influenced by this article and the tone/mood/what-have-you is inherently different between the two (even if the base concept similarities are there) then better to not have that.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
As the author of 2371, I'm conflicted but won't veto.

If I'm misunderstanding my own rights as an author, I apologize for the derpitude.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Author has vetoed this proposal. Timer has been removed.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Okay, so fellow wikiwalker DrBleep pointed out to me the similarity between the effects of SCP-1269 and SCP-2371, with the main differences being in severity of hostility and focus. Its not unlikely that SCPF researchers would look into similarities between the two phenomena. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Exactly three (3) weeks after the appearance of the first letter, SCP-1269's secondary anomalous properties will manifest. Letters will begin appearing daily, with contents becoming increasingly more obsessive and erratic, until the point that letters regress into single sentences. In some occasions, multiple letters will appear within SCP-1269. Additionally, when not under direct supervision of the occupant, SCP-1269 will teleport to a location near the occupant and face them. This location will be partially obstructed, such as behind windows, cracked doors, and shower curtains. Under special circumstances, such as during sleep or when obscured by darkness, SCP-1269 will teleport near the occupant without obstruction. Whether or not the occupant relocates SCP-1269, the object will continue to teleport between a range of one (1) to five (5) times every twelve (12) hours. SCP-1269 will not follow the occupant off the property, and all anomalous properties will cease manifesting after the occupant has either moved or expired.

To this:

Exactly three (3) weeks after the appearance of the first letter, SCP-1269's secondary anomalous properties will manifest. Letters will begin appearing daily, with contents becoming increasingly more obsessive and erratic, until the point that letters regress into single sentences. In some occasions, multiple letters will appear within SCP-1269. Additionally, when not under direct supervision of the occupant, SCP-1269 will teleport to a location near the occupant and face them. This location will be partially obstructed, such as behind windows, cracked doors, and shower curtains. Under special circumstances, such as during sleep or when obscured by darkness, SCP-1269 will teleport near the occupant without obstruction. Whether or not the occupant relocates SCP-1269, the object will continue to teleport between a range of one (1) to five (5) times every twelve (12) hours. SCP-1269 will not follow the occupant off the property, and all anomalous properties will cease manifesting after the occupant has either moved or expired1.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarities between these effects and those observed in SCP-2371 have been noted. Investigation into whether the two anomalies share a similar origin or are variations on the same anomalous phenomena are ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One or several images in this article have been removed due to non-compliance with the Creative Commons license. They may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact apoplexic or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.


This automated message was sent on behalf of apoplexic. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact apoplexic in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an amazing idea. I would like to see more tests. like some have mentioned I would like to see what would happen if the woman wrote back. It would be fun if there was different reactions to things like the woman writing the response herself or the letters being written by the researchers. Also the reactions to acceptance of the mailbox's feelings and rejection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yandere…. mailbox

wat

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems to be a mistake. Fixed, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why are there several "96" dates attached to the letters while the first three were supposedly received in "98" ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SHE ONLY SEES THE GOOD IN PEOPLE AND YOU ARE FULL OF SHIT YOU FUCKER"

That line, written by a mailbox, is what sold me. I am at work and doing my level best to maintain my composure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where fixed. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent, but it's missing a "where" in the containment procedures, and I'd like to see more testing. Still, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is very unique and different. That is why I like it and it's is one of my favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So much upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dammit I love this SCP… UPVOTE +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hilarious with a dash of Fridge Horror? Take my upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does it only start obsessing with single women? It seems to have no problem with killing men once it starts obsessing.

Woohoo! :D

Anyway, Wilt prompted me to add more background on how I came up with this SCP, so…

is background
yay background!

This is what happened the day I thought of the idea.

I had originally agreed to meet up with a friend to go buy snacks together at the nearby student store. I happen to live on campus in a dorm, so I took a nap while my friend attended her last class of the day. She told me when her class would end, so I set an alarm for about five minutes before that time, so I could let her into the dorm building.

When I woke up, I checked my phone. I had five missed calls, two voicemails, and ten text messages, all of which increased in creepiness as I read on. Apparently, my friend's class was cancelled, so she wandered around the campus grounds waiting for me to contact her back. When I finally called her, she had been waiting outside my dorm building for quite some time, and gotten bored.

She mentioned something about staring at your window wondering if you were ever going to acknowledge me and afterwards I thought the entire experience was rather interesting, if unsettling. So I mentioned making an SCP involving similarly creepy (though attempted to be heartfelt) messages to Wilt, and we started working on the idea.

Wilt obviously did the large bulk of writing… mainly due to me being ridiculously with college stuffs. However, he did end up posting it on Valentine's Day, which was great. :D

It was awesome collaborating (I'm sorry it took so long on my part D:) with you, Wilt! :>
Congrats! Great SCP. I lovegod damn pun it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made a minor grammar edit. Upvoted with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would be very appreciative of such a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may try my had at writing a tale about this, I have the worst best idea…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did I miss this one? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. Well done. +1, and I laughed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this. It has so many elements that could make it sound too much like something else (anomalous mailbox, anomalous anonymous letters, a teleporting object)— but the imagery is what really pulls it through. Just the right mixture of absurd and disturbing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it might be worth explaining that scenario in the Description, even if it is only one line somewhere

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See the above question about two women.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if lesbians occupied the house?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stalker mailbox… I can dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …yet. It has never tried to injure the object of its affections yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most likely. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would almost swear you got some of those letters from my out… I mean inbox… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a female, an SCP like this makes me VERY uncomfortable. It's so unassuming and unnoticeable and the letters would easily be attributed to a (human) crazy stalker, maybe the mailbox moving around could possibly be seen as the stalker trying to get the victim to write back and then gets progressively scarier as it would seem the stalker can somehow get inside the house (but that seems like a stretch, at that point I think most people would think that it's more than that I guess). Even knowing that the victim wouldn't be harmed in any way, the fact that a male significant other could be killed, mutilated, and thrown into a dumpster without any way of knowing how is scary enough. You touched on a nice point there too, the mailbox's jealousy, and how stalkers can and sometimes do try to "get rid of competition" and man that's the one scariest thing to me about stalkers.

+++++1, great job, would upvote again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that why there was a pause between the D-class moving in with the current lady and his disappearance? The mailbox was trying to hold back but all the violent emotions pent up until they burst over?

That sort of makes it even creepier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No activation. Too potentially dangerous and preventable, and generally SCP-1269 won't wanna go killin' people, specifically women.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like this. What happens if two women are living in the house?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I used to have a stalker ex at one point, and oh god why did you drag up those feelings my shoulders are cold and burning and gahhh take my goddamn upvote you bastards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you like animate inanimate objects like this, you're gonna love the next collab Roget and I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was just "Do you like Hawaiian Sweet Bread?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same diff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, but it's deliberately ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it definitely involved Hawaiian Sweet Bread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the creepy stalker behavior, and the letters make it even better. I'm actually rather glad that it seems to be the mailbox itself causing the events, rather than just being the conduit for something else, as I initially assumed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hawaiian Sweet Bread, I believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this one, especially when it starts swearing out inanimate objects. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, don't love it. I feel that additional teleporting effects are a bit contrived, would have preferred this thing not being so predictably malvolent.

Another simmilar article: SCP-1204

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What was the other one we came up with? Something with food?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would probably start up to its next stage as soon as it received the letter, start sending letters more frequently, and teleport more frequently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They aren't left in it, they're painted on.

..and covered in painted-on words and phrases.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has never tried to injure them, no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that was the one I felt it was really similar to. Incidentally, Scantron, do you know what's really going on with 1868?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, though I am wondering something. What would happen if she tried to write back? leave messages in the empty mailbox and all that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I sort of like this, but the similarity to SCP-1868, along with the aforementioned omnipotence of it, makes me hesitate. I sort of feel like the letters left in the beat-up mailboxes detracts from the piece, too. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems pretty omnipotent for a mailbox.

Just to clarify, the mailbox's target is in no actual danger? Have they tried trying to injure her?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Happy Valentine's Day, all!

Bit of background: I originally came up with the idea one day when I left my phone to charge while I napped and a friend of mine wanted to hang out. Thing was, her class got out an hour early, so when I woke up, I had a lot of creepy joke-texts that all ended "call me xoxoxo". It was interesting.

I'll type out more after I rest my arm. I got a blood test today. :|

Edit: Enterkeynoididntwanttosubmitthatjustyet
Many thanks to Wilt for writing this up. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love all the subtle leads in this article. Got my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cheers.
Happy Valentine's Day, Teedles.
